title: Preparing People
text:
Preparting People

Preparing people and the organization for Augmented Analytics means building both the skills and the culture needed to embed analytics into daily decision-making. Leaders must champion data-driven practices, analytics translators must bridge business and technical teams, and employees need at least analytics awareness to trust and act on insights. A Center of Excellence (CoE), executive sponsorship, and structured training ensure that AA is not just a technology upgrade but an organizational transformation that empowers all roles to benefit from data in their workflows.
Focus on roles (leaders, translators, users, professionals) and personas to structure the analytics transformation.
Build both technical readiness (data infrastructure, processes) and human readiness (mindset, skills, awareness).
Establish a Center of Excellence (CoE) to drive transformation, ideally business-anchored and federated over time.

A CoE is an organizational structure set up to concentrate expertise, best practices, and resources around a specific domain, technology, or strategic priority. Its main purpose is to drive consistency, innovation, and efficiency across an organization.



Tailoring Augmented Analytics for Different Organizational Roles

Analytics Leader – Champions data-driven culture, aligns insights with strategy.
Analytics Translator – Bridges gap between business and technical teams, ensures use cases deliver value.
Analytics User – Consumes insights for better decisions; majority of workforce.
Analytics Professional – Technical experts building and maintaining analytics solutions.




Figure 4-1.Analytics transformation personas in the three skill categories (Weber & Zwingmann, 2024)





Figure 4-2. The four top-level roles in the analytics transformation (Weber & Zwingmann, 2024)




Center of Excellence (CoE)

A CoE is a unit dedicated to organizing the analytics transformation.
During the Data Active maturity phase, it collects and connects all the loose threads of analytics activities that emerge.

Purpose: Organize and guide analytics transformation, connect business units, build culture.

Core responsibilities:

Promoting data literacy and liberalization.
Building quick-win use cases (“lighthouse” projects).
Creating shared governance and culture.
Supporting business units and connecting their strategies.
Inspiring people across the company to take ownership of analytics





Figure 4-4. A center of excellence drawn from both IT and business units (Weber & Zwingmann, 2024)




Approaches to Organizing a CoE

Decentralized Approach

Common in early-stage, Data Reactive organizations.
Business units develop analytics independently, often using ad hoc tools (Excel, VBA, Python, R, etc.).
Pros: High independence and agility within business domains.
Cons: Lack of governance, scalability issues, silos, and inefficiencies.
Bottom line: Tempting but unsustainable without stronger coordination.

Centralized Approach

Common in smaller organizations or when business units lack analytical skills.
A single CoE or IT unit manages analytics, reporting, and infrastructure.
Pros: Efficient, well-designed solutions and processes.
Cons: Bottlenecks, slow responsiveness, weak business insight, lack of shared ownership.
Bottom line: Works for early stages or small orgs but limits growth and responsiveness.




Federated Approach

A hybrid model that balances centralized expertise with decentralized ownership.
CoE starts as a strong driver, then gradually shifts responsibility to business units as data literacy grows.
Pros:

Encourages shared responsibility and democratization of data.
Business units handle routine analytics while the CoE tackles advanced challenges.
Builds a stronger data culture and faster responsiveness.

Cons: Requires strong governance, collaboration, and patience.
Bottom line: The most effective model for achieving Data Progressive (stage 3) maturity.




Difficulties Preparing People

Inherent bias

Many of us are unaware of our knowledge gaps because we are inherently biased. We don’t know what we don’t know.

Perceived solvability

People often assume that complex problems can be solved somehow, without actually tackling them and testing the approaches. This is particularly true in the AI environment, where the underlying technology accomplishes wonderful things in vaguely understood ways. It’s important that people understand how problem solving actually works, because finding a solution usually involves a lot of hard work.

Learned helplessness

Repeated mistakes or negative experiences can lead individuals to believe that they cannot change, creating a state of “learned helplessness.” Organizations must instill the belief that effort can lead to improvement. With the advent of analytics, there are more opportunities than ever to demonstrate this in action.




Cultivating Data Literacy

Key Competencies

Analytics Awareness

Helps non-specialists understand data processes, quality, visualization, and core AI/ML concepts.
Fosters effective communication with data professionals and realistic expectations for AI projects.

Storytelling with Data

Translates complex insights into clear narratives for different audiences.
Requires mastering visualization, decluttering, and tailoring tone/context to make data compelling and actionable.

Data-Driven Management

Managers must commit to analytics strategy, ensure data integrity, and integrate analytics into decision-making.
Focus on aligning analytics with business goals, fostering ownership, and promoting a culture of continuous learning.

Leading in the Age of AI

Leaders need to progress from AI awareness to active engagement.
Competencies include identifying AI opportunities, managing AI project lifecycles, and integrating AI into business strategy.
Goal: build organizational capacity to leverage AI for transformation and innovation.






Figure 4-7. Four key aspects of data literacy (Weber & Zwingmann, 2024)




Enablement Programs

Analytics adoption requires different levels of data literacy depending on role.

Analytics translators need high literacy to identify and develop use cases.
Analytics users need analytics awareness—a general understanding of data’s value, processes, and visualization—to effectively use workflows without being experts.
Analytics leaders and practitioners need awareness, storytelling, and role-specific competencies.

For Leaders

Initial workshops, executive roundtables, ThinkLabs, annual leadership summits.

For Translators

Deep-dives into tools/methods, cross-disciplinary ThinkLabs, mentoring, project presentations

For Professionals

Ongoing technical training (SQL, Python, R, statistics, ML).




Workflow Augmentation

A workflow is the repeatable sequence of steps, tasks, and decisions employees follow to achieve a business outcome.
Augmented Analytics succeeds only when insights are embedded inside workflows, not delivered separately in dashboards or reports.



Types of Workflow Augmentation

Fixed-Rule, High-Confidence Augmentation

Automated actions when preset conditions are met.
Works best for routine, well-defined tasks.
Example: routing claims or prepopulating CRM entries.





Idea & Insight Enrichment

Supports human decision-making with predictions, benchmarks, and visualizations.
Acts as a “copilot,” surfacing trends or comparisons.
Example: benchmarking risks in insurance underwriting.

Conversational Augmentation

Uses GenAI/LLMs in interactive assistants.
Example: GitHub Copilot for coding, or AI chat tools for summarizing contracts.

Contextual (Adaptive) Augmentation

Learns from users’ context, tailoring responses to role, history, or task.
Example: adaptive email or recommendation systems.

Collaborative Augmentation

Enhances teamwork through shared dashboards, repositories, and chatbots.
Example: Azure chatbot integrated into Microsoft Teams for compliance support.




Finding Workflows to Augment

Use the Analytics Use-Case Approach: a structured pipeline from idea → concept → proof of concept → prototype → pilot → product.

Each phase evaluates risk, ROI, and maturity before committing resources.
Success depends on validating assumptions iteratively.

Balancing Automation & Integration

Workflows vary by automation (how much is done without humans) and integration (how deeply embedded in systems).

Four categories:



Four Categories


Best to start with assistants and copilots before scaling to autopilots and agents.



The Use-Case Library

A central repository that tracks all analytics use cases from idea to deployment.
Benefits: transparency, monitoring, innovation sharing, cultural motivation, visibility of stakeholders.
Often includes Kanban boards, evaluation matrices, and documentation to foster collaboration and standardization.




Kanban Board (Businessmap, n.d.)




Technical & Organizational Requirements

APIs & microservices (REST, Python, R) are recommended for flexible, vendor-independent integration.

REST stands for Representational State Transfer. It’s an architectural style for designing web services and APIs that allow systems to communicate over the internet.

It decouples front-end and back-end, letting different apps (web, mobile, IoT) consume the same API.

In the Augmented Workflows chapter, REST APIs are recommended to integrate analytics into business workflows because they are modular, secure, and reusable.

Challenges: legacy systems, IT dependencies, governance, security, and compliance.
Analytics contracts (similar to data contracts) are proposed to ensure discoverability, trust, interoperability, and responsible use.

title: Using Git and GitHub
text:
Setting Up GitHub

To get started with GitHub, follow these steps:

Create a free account at github.com.
If working locally, install Git from gitforwindows.org.
Configure your identity so commits are attributed correctly:


git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@example.com"


Optionally, install GitHub Desktop for a graphical interface instead of the command line.
Set VS Code as your preferred editor for writing and reviewing code alongside Git.

title: Using Git and GitHub
text:
GitHub Copilot Autofill

To enable Copilot’s inline ghost-text suggestions as you type in VS Code:

Open VS Code Settings.
Search for Copilot: Inline Suggest.
Ensure that Editor › Inline Suggest: Enabled is checked.

title: Using Git and GitHub
text:
Creating Your First Repository
To create a new repository on GitHub, go to your profile, click New Repository, and give it a name. Choose whether it should be public or private. Do not add a README at this stage — since you will be pushing an existing project from VS Code, it is better to start with a blank repository to avoid merge conflicts on the first push.
Alternatively, you can create a repository locally by making a folder and running git init, then linking it to a new GitHub repository. The full sequence of commands for an initial commit from VS Code is:

git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit"
git branch -M main
git remote add origin https://github.com/USERNAME/REPO_NAME.git
git push -u origin main

Once your files are committed and pushed, notice the Source Control button on the left sidebar of VS Code — it displays the number of pending changes waiting to be committed.

title: Using Git and GitHub
text:
Best Practices
Adopting good habits early makes collaboration and troubleshooting much easier:

Write clear commit messages — descriptive messages like "Fix login bug" are far more useful than vague ones like "stuff".
Pull frequently — run git pull origin main before starting new work to stay synchronized with your team’s latest changes.
Use .gitignore — exclude sensitive credentials, environment files, and large unnecessary files from being tracked or uploaded.
Keep the main branch stable — develop new features on separate branches and only merge into main when code is tested and ready.
Use Issues and Project Boards — GitHub’s built-in task tracking tools help teams coordinate work and maintain visibility across a project.

title: Using Git and GitHub
text:
Troubleshooting Common Issues
A few problems come up regularly when working with Git and GitHub:

Merge conflicts — occur when two contributors change the same section of code. Git will flag the conflict and ask you to resolve it manually before merging.
Authentication errors — typically resolved by setting up an SSH key or a personal access token in your GitHub account settings.
Forgetting to pull — always run git pull before pushing to avoid conflicts caused by out-of-sync local and remote histories.
Accidental commits — use git reset to unstage changes or git revert to safely undo a commit without rewriting history.

text: Render Dashboard

title: Using Git and GitHub
text:
Before You Deploy: Required Files
Before Render can build your application, your GitHub repository must contain three specific files. Render reads these to understand how to install your dependencies and run your app. Without them, the deployment will fail.

requirements.txt
This file tells Render which Python packages to install. Each package goes on its own line, optionally pinned to a specific version. For a standard Dash application, your requirements.txt should include at minimum:
dash
pandas
plotly
gunicorn

gunicorn is required for Render to serve your app in production. It is not needed when running locally, but without it Render will not know how to start your server.



app.py (or your main Python file)
This is the entry point of your Dash application — the file Render will execute to start the server. Make sure your file includes the following line to expose the server object, and that the run block at the bottom uses app.run() rather than the deprecated app.run_server():

server = app.server  # required for gunicorn

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(debug=False)


app.run_server() is deprecated. Use app.run() instead. Set debug=False for production — leaving debug mode on in a live environment is a security risk.



render.yaml (optional but recommended)
This file gives Render explicit instructions for how to build and start your app. While Render can often detect settings automatically, providing a render.yaml makes your deployment more reliable and portable. A minimal example:
services:
  - type: web
    name: my-dash-app
    env: python
    buildCommand: "pip install -r requirements.txt"
    startCommand: "gunicorn app:server"
Replace my-dash-app with your app’s name and confirm that app:server matches your filename (app.py) and the server object name (server = app.server).


Deployment Checklist
Before pushing to GitHub and triggering a Render deploy, confirm the following:

requirements.txt is present and includes gunicorn.
app.run_server() has been replaced with app.run(debug=False).
server = app.server is defined in your app.py so gunicorn can find the entry point.
Your repository is linked to Render and auto-deploy is enabled.
No secrets or API keys are hardcoded — use Render’s environment variable settings instead.

title: Writing Callbacks and Accessing APIs
text:
Callbacks in Dash: The Basics

# Callback syntax template
from dash import Input, Output

@app.callback(
    Output(component_id, component_property),  # where to send the result
    Input(component_id, component_property)    # what triggers the function
)
def function_name(input_object):
    # process the input
    return output_object   # must match the Output(s) declared above

Breaking down the parts:

@app.callback — the decorator that registers this function as a callback. It tells Dash: “when the Input changes, run the function below and send the return value to the Output.”
Input — specifies which component and which property will trigger the function when it changes.
Output — specifies which component and which property should be updated with the function’s return value.
component_id — the id string you assigned to a layout component (e.g., id="my-dropdown").
component_property — a specific property of that component (e.g., "value" for a dropdown, "children" for a Div, "figure" for a Graph).
crumbs: 13  Writing Callbacks and Accessing APIs

title: Writing Callbacks and Accessing APIs
text:
The Function





The function arguments match the order of the Inputs, and the return values match the order of the Outputs:

function_name(input_object) receives the current value from the Input.
Inside the function, you process that value and produce the desired output.
return output_object sends the processed output back to the specified Output.
crumbs: 13  Writing Callbacks and Accessing APIs

title: Writing Callbacks and Accessing APIs
text:
Multiple Inputs and Outputs
A single callback can handle multiple inputs and outputs:

Multiple Inputs — the function runs whenever any of the listed inputs change. The arguments arrive in the same order the Inputs are declared.
Multiple Outputs — return a tuple or list with one value per Output, in the same order they are declared.


@app.callback(
    [Output('graph',   'figure'),    # output 1: update the chart
     Output('summary', 'children')], # output 2: update the text summary
    [Input('dropdown', 'value'),     # input 1: dropdown selection
     Input('slider',   'value')]     # input 2: slider position
)
def update_dashboard(selected_category, slider_value):
    # arguments match Input order: selected_category ← dropdown, slider_value ← slider
    fig  = make_figure(selected_category, slider_value)
    text = f"Showing: {selected_category} | Threshold: {slider_value}"
    return fig, text   # return values match Output order

title: Writing Callbacks and Accessing APIs
text:
Using dcc — Dash Core Components
dcc is short for dash.dcc (Dash Core Components). It is a module in Dash that provides interactive UI elements including input boxes, sliders, dropdowns, date pickers, and graphs. These components are the primary building blocks for user-driven interactivity in a Dash app.
crumbs: 13  Writing Callbacks and Accessing APIs

title: Writing Callbacks and Accessing APIs
text:
Callback Example

The complete app below creates a text input box whose value is mirrored in real time to a large display Div — the simplest possible demonstration of a callback in action.

# callback_example.py
from dash import Dash, html, dcc, Input, Output, callback

app = Dash(__name__)
app.title = "Callback Example"

app.layout = html.Div(
    style={"maxWidth": 900, "margin": "40px auto", "fontFamily": "Georgia, serif"},
    children=[
        html.H1("Callback Example"),
        html.Ul([
            html.Li(["Input box's ", html.Code("value"), " property updates output text"])
        ]),
        dcc.Input(
            id="text-in",           # ID used in the callback Input()
            type="text",
            placeholder="type here…",
            style={"width": "100%", "fontSize": "48px", "padding": "8px"},
        ),
        html.Div(id="text-out",     # ID used in the callback Output()
                 style={"fontSize": "64px", "marginTop": "20px"}),
    ],
)

@callback(
    Output(component_id="text-out", component_property="children"),
    Input(component_id="text-in",  component_property="value")
)
def show_text(value):
    return f"Text: {value or ''}"   # value or '' prevents an error when the box is empty

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(debug=True)
crumbs: 13  Writing Callbacks and Accessing APIs

title: Writing Callbacks and Accessing APIs
text:
dcc.Input
dcc.Input renders an HTML input box inside your app. The most important property is value — it holds whatever the user has typed and is what you connect to a callback.

dcc.Input(
    id="text-in",        # unique ID for callback wiring
    type="text",         # "text", "number", "password", "email", etc.
    placeholder="type here…",
    debounce=False,      # if True, only fires callback when user presses Enter or clicks away
    style={"width": "100%", "fontSize": "48px", "padding": "8px"},
)


children is the default first positional argument for dash.html components, so html.Div("Hello") is equivalent to html.Div(children="Hello"). You can omit children= when passing it as the first argument.

title: Writing Callbacks and Accessing APIs
section: Making a Callback Using NASA’s API
text:
Making a Callback Using NASA’s API

Overview
The goal is to build a Dash app that connects to NASA’s Astronomy Picture of the Day (APOD) API, letting users pick a date and instantly see the image or video NASA published for that day, along with its title and description.
APOD is a free, public API — no sign-up required for testing with the DEMO_KEY, though the demo key has rate limits (30 requests/hour). For heavier use, register for a free key at api.nasa.gov.


Imports and Constants

import datetime as dt
import requests
from dash import Dash, html, dcc, Input, Output, exceptions

API_KEY  = "DEMO_KEY"                           # replace with your own for higher rate limits
APOD_URL = "https://api.nasa.gov/planetary/apod"
MIN_DATE = dt.date(1995, 6, 16)                 # first APOD was published June 16, 1995
TODAY    = dt.date.today()                      # computed at runtime, not hardcoded




Setting the Layout
The layout defines what the user sees. Static in structure but contains placeholder components (identified by id) that callbacks will populate with live data.

app = Dash(__name__)
app.title = "NASA APOD"

app.layout = html.Div(
    style={"margin": "0 auto", "padding": 20},
    children=[
        html.H1("NASA Astronomy Picture of the Day"),

        # Date picker: user selects which date to view
        dcc.DatePickerSingle(
            id="apod-date",
            min_date_allowed=MIN_DATE,       # earliest valid date
            max_date_allowed=TODAY,          # cannot pick a future date
            date=TODAY,                      # default selection: today
            display_format="YYYY-MM-DD",
            clearable=False,                 # prevent the user from clearing the date
        ),

        # Spinner shown while media is loading; wraps the image/video placeholder
        dcc.Loading(html.Div(id="media",
                             style={"marginTop": 16, "textAlign": "center"})),

        # Placeholder for the text description
        html.Div(id="caption"),
    ],
)

Key layout components:

dcc.DatePickerSingle — renders a calendar date picker. Its date property (a string in YYYY-MM-DD format) is what the callback listens to.
dcc.Loading — wraps a component and shows a loading spinner automatically whenever a callback is updating that component.
id="media" and id="caption" — these are empty placeholders; callbacks will fill them with content.



Making the Callback

@app.callback(
    [Output("media",   "children"),   # will receive the image or video component
     Output("caption", "children")],  # will receive the explanation text
    Input("apod-date", "date"),        # triggers when the selected date changes
)



The show_apod Function

def show_apod(date_str):
    # Guard: if no date is selected, do nothing
    if not date_str:
        raise exceptions.PreventUpdate

    # Convert the date string to a Python date object
    # [:10] slices "2024-01-15T00:00:00" down to "2024-01-15"
    try:
        date_obj = dt.date.fromisoformat(date_str[:10])
    except Exception:
        return "Invalid date.", ""

    # Build query parameters and call the API
    params = {"api_key": API_KEY, "date": date_obj.isoformat()}
    try:
        r = requests.get(APOD_URL, params=params, timeout=10)
        r.raise_for_status()   # raises an exception if status code is not 200
        data = r.json()        # parse JSON response into a Python dictionary
    except requests.RequestException as e:
        return f"API error: {e}", ""

What each part does:

exceptions.PreventUpdate — a special Dash exception that stops the callback from running without updating any outputs. Use this instead of returning None when there is genuinely nothing to update.
date_str[:10] — the date picker sometimes includes a time component (T00:00:00). Slicing to the first 10 characters ensures we always get just YYYY-MM-DD.
r.raise_for_status() — raises a requests.HTTPError if the server returned an error code (4xx or 5xx). Without this, a failed request would appear to succeed.
timeout=10 — prevents the app from hanging indefinitely if the API is slow. 10 seconds is a reasonable threshold.



Rendering the Media

    # Extract fields safely using .get() with defaults
    media_type  = data.get("media_type", "")   # "image" or "video"
    url         = data.get("url", "")
    title       = data.get("title", "APOD")
    explanation = data.get("explanation", "")

    # Render the appropriate component based on media type
    if media_type == "image":
        media = html.Img(
            src=url,
            style={"maxWidth": "100%", "borderRadius": 8},
            alt=title   # alt text for accessibility
        )
    elif media_type == "video":
        media = html.Iframe(
            src=url,
            style={"width": "100%", "height": 500, "border": 0}
        )
    else:
        media = html.Div("Unsupported media type.")

    header = html.H3(f"{title} — {date_obj.isoformat()}",
                     style={"marginBottom": 8})

    # Return TWO values — one for each Output in the callback
    return html.Div([header, media]), explanation

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(debug=True)

Why .get() with defaults? The NASA API may not include every field for every date (especially very early APOD entries). Using .get("key", default) prevents KeyError exceptions when a field is absent — a form of defensive programming essential for any code calling external APIs.
The function returns two values separated by a comma, matching the two Outputs declared in @app.callback. The order matters: the first return value goes to "media", the second to "caption".

title: Writing Callbacks and Accessing APIs
text:
Setting the Layout
The layout is the blueprint of your app’s user interface — it defines what components appear on the page and how they are arranged. The layout is static in structure but holds placeholder components (identified by id) that callbacks update dynamically. You can nest components, style them, and set their initial properties here.

html.Div(...) — main container with max width, centered on the page, and padding.
html.H1(...) — page title.
dcc.DatePickerSingle(...) — lets the user select a date between MIN_DATE and TODAY; defaults to today.
dcc.Loading(...) — shows a spinner while content loads; wraps the Div(id="media") placeholder where the NASA image or video will appear.
html.Div(id="caption") — placeholder for the APOD text description.


app = Dash(__name__)
app.title = "NASA APOD"

app.layout = html.Div(
    style={"margin": "0 auto", "padding": 20},
    children=[
        html.H1("NASA Astronomy Picture of the Day"),
        dcc.DatePickerSingle(
            id="apod-date",
            min_date_allowed=MIN_DATE,
            max_date_allowed=TODAY,
            date=TODAY,
            display_format="YYYY-MM-DD",
            clearable=False,
        ),
        dcc.Loading(html.Div(id="media", style={"marginTop": 16,
                                                "textAlign": "center"})),
        html.Div(id="caption"),
    ],
)
crumbs: 13  Writing Callbacks and Accessing APIs

title: Writing Callbacks and Accessing APIs
text:
Making the Callback
@app.callback(...) is a decorator that tells Dash to run the function below it whenever the specified Inputs change, and to send the return values to the specified Outputs.
This callback has two Outputs:

Output("media", "children") — updates the image or video placeholder.
Output("caption", "children") — updates the text description.

And one Input:

Input("apod-date", "date") — the function runs whenever the selected date changes.


@app.callback(
    [Output("media", "children"), Output("caption", "children")],
    Input("apod-date", "date"),
)
crumbs: 13  Writing Callbacks and Accessing APIs

title: Writing Callbacks and Accessing APIs
text:
The show_apod Function

def show_apod(date_str):
    if not date_str:
        raise exceptions.PreventUpdate

    try:
        date_obj = dt.date.fromisoformat(date_str[:10])
    except Exception:
        return "Invalid date.", ""

    params = {"api_key": API_KEY, "date": date_obj.isoformat()}
    try:
        r = requests.get(APOD_URL, params=params, timeout=10)
        r.raise_for_status()
        data = r.json()
    except requests.RequestException as e:
        return f"API error: {e}", ""

This function takes the user-selected date, validates it, calls the NASA APOD API, and returns either the media content and caption or an error message. Here is what each part does:

def show_apod(date_str) — the callback function; receives the selected date as a string.
if not date_str: raise exceptions.PreventUpdate — if no date is provided, stop the callback without changing anything on the page.
dt.date.fromisoformat(date_str[:10]) — converts the date string (YYYY-MM-DD) into a Python date object. The [:10] slice ensures only the date portion is used regardless of how the date picker formats its value.
except Exception — if conversion fails, return "Invalid date." for the media section and an empty string for the caption.
params = {"api_key": ..., "date": ...} — builds the query parameters for the NASA API request.
requests.get(APOD_URL, params=params, timeout=10) — sends a GET request to NASA’s API, allowing up to 10 seconds before timing out.
r.raise_for_status() — raises an error if the HTTP response status is not 200 (OK).
data = r.json() — parses the API response JSON into a Python dictionary.
except requests.RequestException as e — if any network or request error occurs, returns an error message for the media section.
crumbs: 13  Writing Callbacks and Accessing APIs

title: Writing Callbacks and Accessing APIs
section: Rendering the Media and Returning Results
text:
Rendering the Media and Returning Results

    media_type  = data.get("media_type", "")
    url         = data.get("url", "")
    title       = data.get("title", "APOD")
    explanation = data.get("explanation", "")

    if media_type == "image":
        media = html.Img(src=url, style={"maxWidth": "100%", "borderRadius": 8}, alt=title)
    elif media_type == "video":
        media = html.Iframe(src=url, style={"width": "100%", "height": 500, "border": 0})
    else:
        media = html.Div("Unsupported media type.")

    header = html.H3(f"{title} — {date_obj.isoformat()}", style={"marginBottom": 8})
    return html.Div([header, media]), explanation

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(debug=True)

The function extracts fields from the API response using .get() so a safe default is returned if any key is missing:

media_type — "image" or "video" (empty string if missing).
url — link to the media file.
title — the APOD title (defaults to "APOD" if missing).
explanation — the description text (empty string if missing).

Based on media_type, the function renders the appropriate component:

If "image" → creates an html.Img with the URL and styling.
If "video" → creates an html.Iframe to embed the video.
Otherwise → shows a Div indicating the media type is unsupported.

Finally, the function returns two values to match the callback’s two Outputs: a Div containing the header and media component, and the explanation text for the caption section.

title: Creating a Grid Layout in Dash
text:
What Is Bootstrap?
Bootstrap is a popular front-end framework that provides a responsive grid layout, prebuilt UI components, and utility classes. Dash Bootstrap Components (dash-bootstrap-components, imported as dbc) makes Bootstrap available inside Dash apps, including:

dbc.Row() — a horizontal container for columns.
dbc.Col() — where content is placed; only Col components should be direct children of a Row.

Bootstrap divides each row into 12 equal units. You control column width with md=N, where N is how many of those 12 units the column occupies (e.g., md=4 = one-third width, md=6 = half width).

text:
Making a Two-Column Layout
dash_bootstrap_components lets you apply a full Bootstrap theme via an external stylesheet, instantly giving your app Bootstrap’s responsive grid, typography, and utility classes — with no custom CSS required. You can swap themes (e.g., dbc.themes.COSMO, LUX, MATERIA, CYBORG) for a new look while still overriding specific rules in your own assets/style.css.

from dash import Dash, html
import dash_bootstrap_components as dbc

app.title = "Multi Layout App"

Embedded styles are Python dictionaries attached directly via the style prop — useful for quick, local styling and prototypes. For shared, consistent styling across a multi-page app, move rules to an external stylesheet in assets/ and reserve embedded styles for small overrides.

box_style = {
    "border": "2px solid black",
    "borderRadius": "5px",
    "margin": "5px",
    "padding": "20px",
    "backgroundColor": "#e0f7fa"
}


app.layout = dbc.Container([
    # First row: full width
    dbc.Row(
        dbc.Col(html.Div("Top Row (Full Width)", style=box_style), width=12)),
    # Second row: two equal columns
    dbc.Row([
        dbc.Col(html.Div("Left Column", style=box_style)),
        dbc.Col(html.Div("Right Column", style=box_style))
    ]),
    # Footer: full width
    dbc.Row(dbc.Col(html.Div("Footer (Full Width)", style=box_style), width=12))
], fluid=True)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(debug=True)

import requests
import pandas as pd                          # data frame and manipulation
from dash import Dash, html, dcc, Input, Output
import dash_bootstrap_components as dbc      # Bootstrap components
import plotly.express as px                  # data visualization

# ---------- City coordinates ----------
CITY_COORDS = {
    "Williamsburg":    (37.2707,  -76.7075),
    "Richmond":        (37.5407,  -77.4360),
    "Virginia Beach":  (36.8529,  -75.9780),
    "Roanoke":         (37.27097, -79.94143),
    "Charlottesville": (38.0293,  -78.4767)
}

title: Creating a Grid Layout in Dash
text:
Fetching the Hourly Temperature
The fetch_hourly_temp(lat, lon) function takes a latitude and longitude and returns a Pandas DataFrame containing the next 48 hours of hourly temperatures.

Builds the API URL — uses the Open-Meteo forecast endpoint with query parameters: latitude, longitude, hourly=temperature_2m, forecast_days=2, and timezone=auto. An endpoint is the specific URL where an API resource can be accessed.
Calls the API safely — requests.get(..., timeout=15) and r.raise_for_status() surface any HTTP errors immediately.
Parses the JSON response — extracts the "hourly" block and builds a DataFrame with two columns: time and temp_C. The _2m suffix indicates temperature is measured 2 meters above ground level.
Cleans timestamps — converts the time column to proper datetime objects with pd.to_datetime().


def fetch_hourly_temp(lat: float, lon: float) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Fetch next-48-hours hourly temperature from Open-Meteo."""
    url = (
        "https://api.open-meteo.com/v1/forecast"
        f"?latitude={lat}&longitude={lon}"
        "&hourly=temperature_2m&forecast_days=2&timezone=auto"
    )
    r = requests.get(url, timeout=15)
    r.raise_for_status()
    data = r.json()["hourly"]
    df = pd.DataFrame({"time": data["time"], "temp_C": data["temperature_2m"]})
    df["time"] = pd.to_datetime(df["time"])
    return df

text:
CSS: Using IDs vs. Classes
In a multi-page Dash app, a single external stylesheet in assets/ is best practice: it centralizes all styles, ensures a consistent look and feel across pages, and keeps page files clean. Updating CSS in one place updates every page automatically.

ID (id) — identifies one unique element on a page; in Dash it is also used to wire callbacks. CSS selector: #my-id. IDs have higher specificity than classes and win style conflicts.
Class (class) — labels elements for reusable styling and can be applied to many elements. CSS selector: .btn-strong. Classes have lower specificity than IDs.

Prefer classes for reusable styles; reserve IDs for unique elements or callback targets.

Setting the KPI Cards and Chart

# KPI cards (top row)
def kpi_card(title, id_):
    return dbc.Card(
        dbc.CardBody([
            html.H6(title, className="text-muted mb-1"),
            html.H3(id=id_, className="mb-0"),
        ]),
        className="h-100",
    )

kpi_row = dbc.Row(
    [
        dbc.Col(kpi_card("Current Temp (°C)", "kpi-now"), md=4),
        dbc.Col(kpi_card("Min (°C/48h)",      "kpi-min"), md=4),
        dbc.Col(kpi_card("Max (°C/48h)",      "kpi-max"), md=4),
    ],
    className="g-3 mb-3",
)

# Chart (middle column)
chart_card = dbc.Card([
    dbc.CardHeader("Hourly Temperature (next 48h)"),
    dbc.CardBody(dcc.Graph(id="temp-chart", config={"displayModeBar": False})),
])


kpi_card function — a helper that creates a Bootstrap card for displaying a Key Performance Indicator. It takes a title label and an id_ so the numeric value can be updated by callbacks. className="h-100" ensures the card fills its column height.
kpi_row — a Bootstrap row with three equal columns (md=4 = 4/12 width each): current temperature ("kpi-now"), minimum over 48h ("kpi-min"), and maximum over 48h ("kpi-max"). "g-3 mb-3" adds column gutters and bottom margin.
chart_card — a Bootstrap card housing the temperature line chart. config={"displayModeBar": False} hides the Plotly toolbar.

title: Creating a Grid Layout in Dash
text:
Callbacks and Chart

# ---------- Callbacks ----------
@app.callback(
    [
        Output("temp-chart", "figure"),
        Output("kpi-now",    "children"),
        Output("kpi-min",    "children"),
        Output("kpi-max",    "children"),
        Output("stats-table","children"),
    ],
    [Input("city-dd", "value"), Input("refresh", "n_clicks")],
)
def update(city, _):
    lat, lon = CITY_COORDS[city]
    df = fetch_hourly_temp(lat, lon)

    # KPI values
    now  = df.iloc[0]["temp_C"]
    tmin = df["temp_C"].min()
    tmax = df["temp_C"].max()

    # Chart
    fig = px.line(df, x="time", y="temp_C", markers=True, title=None)
    fig.update_layout(margin=dict(l=10, r=10, t=10, b=10),
                      yaxis_title="°C", xaxis_title="Time")


Five Outputs — the chart figure, current/min/max KPI values, and the stats table HTML.
Two Inputs — the selected city dropdown ("city-dd") and the Refresh button click count ("refresh").
The function looks up the city’s coordinates from CITY_COORDS, calls fetch_hourly_temp(), extracts KPI values from the first row and overall min/max, then builds a Plotly Express line chart with tight margins and labeled axes.

text:
Summary and Return

    # Stats table
    summary = (
        df.assign(Date=df["time"].dt.date)
        .groupby("Date")["temp_C"]
        .agg(["min", "max", "mean"])
        .round(1)
        .rename(columns={"min": "Min °C", "max": "Max °C", "mean": "Avg °C"})
        .reset_index()
    )
    table = dbc.Table.from_dataframe(summary, striped=True, bordered=False, hover=True)

    fmt = lambda x: f"{x:.1f}"
    return fig, fmt(now), fmt(tmin), fmt(tmax), table

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(debug=True)


Stats table creation — extracts the date from "time", groups by date, aggregates min/max/mean temperatures, rounds to 1 decimal, renames columns for display, and converts to a Bootstrap table with striped=True and hover=True.
fmt lambda — a one-line formatting function: lambda x: f"{x:.1f}" returns a number formatted to exactly 1 decimal place.
Return values — the chart, three formatted KPI strings, and the stats table, in the same order as the five declared Outputs.

title: Setting Up Multiple Pages
text:
Place your main entry file (app.py) at the root of the project.
Create a pages/ folder to store your individual page layouts.
Add an assets/ folder for custom CSS or other static files — Dash automatically loads files from this directory.
Optionally include additional helper scripts or configuration files as needed.


my_dash_app/
│
├── app.py
├── pages/
│   ├── home.py
│   ├── page1.py
│   └── page2.py
│
└── assets/
    └── style.css

title: Setting Up Multiple Pages
text:
Initialize the App

import dash
from dash import dcc, html
import dash_bootstrap_components as dbc

# Initialize app
app = dash.Dash(__name__,
                use_pages=True,
                suppress_callback_exceptions=True,
                title="Multi-Page-App")

server = app.server  # required for deployment


use_pages=True — tells Dash to scan the pages/ folder and automatically register any pages it finds.
suppress_callback_exceptions=True — prevents errors when callbacks reference components that live on a different page and are not yet rendered.


The original code had supress_callback_exceptions=True (one p). The correct spelling is suppress_callback_exceptions=True. A misspelled keyword argument is silently ignored by Python, meaning exception suppression would not actually be enabled.

title: Setting Up Multiple Pages
text:
Register Each Page
Each file in pages/ calls dash.register_page() to tell Dash its URL path and display name, then defines a layout variable containing the page’s content.

# home.py
import dash
from dash import html

dash.register_page(__name__, path="/")

layout = html.Div([...])


# page1.py
import dash
from dash import html

dash.register_page(__name__, path="/page1", name="Page 1")

layout = html.Div([...])


# page2.py
import dash
from dash import html

dash.register_page(__name__, path="/page2", name="Page 2")

layout = html.Div([...])


The original code used __name_ (single trailing underscore) instead of __name__ (double underscore). __name__ is a built-in Python variable that holds the module’s name; __name_ is undefined and would raise a NameError. Corrected throughout.

Key points about dash.register_page():

__name__ — ensures the page is uniquely identified by its module.
path="/" — makes this file the home page (root URL).
name="Page 1" — sets the display name used in navigation links.
Each page file needs this call plus a layout variable that defines the page’s content.

title: Setting Up Multiple Pages
text:
Create the Layout in app.py
This layout ties everything together: a navigation bar for switching between pages, and a page container that renders the active page’s content dynamically.

dbc.NavbarSimple — creates a top navigation bar with links to each page.
dbc.NavLink("Home", href="/", active="exact") — defines a clickable link. active="exact" ensures the link is highlighted only when the URL matches exactly, preventing the Home link from appearing active on all pages.
dash.page_container — the placeholder that automatically renders the layout of whichever page the user has navigated to.


Only include app.run(...) in app.py. Do not add a run command in any page file. The application is started once from app.py, which automatically loads all registered pages from pages/.


app.layout = html.Div([
    # Navigation bar
    dbc.NavbarSimple(
        children=[
            dbc.NavLink("Home",   href="/",      active="exact"),
            dbc.NavLink("Page 1", href="/page1", active="exact"),
            dbc.NavLink("Page 2", href="/page2", active="exact"),
        ],
        brand="Multi-Page App",
    ),
    dash.page_container
])

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(debug=True)

section: Add a Callback and API to page2.py
text:
Add a Callback and API to page2.py
Page 2 demonstrates how to wire a callback to a public API inside a registered page:

Layout — a heading, a short description, and a button (id="btn-cat"). A dcc.Loading wrapper shows a spinner while data loads; the result appears in Div(id="cat-fact"). Note there is no app.run(...) here — only in app.py.
Callback wiring — @callback(Output("cat-fact", "children"), Input("btn-cat", "n_clicks"), prevent_initial_call=True). prevent_initial_call=True prevents an API call on first page load; the callback only runs when the button is clicked.
API call — requests.get("https://catfact.ninja/fact", timeout=5) fetches a random cat fact. r.raise_for_status() throws if the HTTP status indicates an error. r.json().get("fact", "No fact found.") extracts the text safely.
Error handling — except requests.RequestException catches network or HTTP timeouts and displays a readable error message.


IDs in the layout (btn-cat, cat-fact) must exactly match the IDs referenced in the callback. Requires pip install requests.


from dash import html, register_page, dcc, callback, Output, Input
import requests

register_page(__name__, path="/page2", name="Page 2")

layout = html.Div([
    html.H2("Page 2", className="page-title"),
    html.P("Click to fetch a random cat fact from a public API",
           className="page-subtitle"),
    html.Button("Get Cat Fact", id="btn-cat", n_clicks=0),
    dcc.Loading(html.Div(id="cat-fact"))
], className="page2-wrap")


@callback(
    Output("cat-fact", "children"),
    Input("btn-cat", "n_clicks"),
    prevent_initial_call=True
)
def get_cat_fact(n):
    try:
        r = requests.get("https://catfact.ninja/fact", timeout=5)
        r.raise_for_status()
        fact = r.json().get("fact", "No fact found.")
        return html.Div(fact)
    except requests.RequestException as e:
        return html.Div(f"Error contacting API: {e}")


The original callback was missing prevent_initial_call=True. Without it, Dash fires the callback immediately on page load with n_clicks=0, triggering an unnecessary API request before the user has interacted with the page.
